key_points: Bleaching agents (16% and 30% hydrogen peroxide) caused a decrease in enamel microhardness and surface roughness compared to initial values.
After a demineralization-remineralization (pH) cycle with sodium fluoride toothpaste, microhardness values increased in bleached groups, but the increase was not statistically significant (P< 0.05).
Surface roughness values increased significantly after the pH cycle, with significant differences between the 4th measurement and other time points (P< 0.05).
FE-SEM analysis at x1,000 and x5,000 magnifications was used to evaluate surface morphology changes.
Remineralizing agents applied during the pH cycle may have a positive effect on enamel microhardness following bleaching.
32 extracted premolar teeth were used; samples were immersed in coffee solution for 1 week to simulate oral discoloration before bleaching.
Bleaching was performed in three sessions of 15 minutes each per manufacturer's instructions.

outcomes.result: Highest initial values; decreased after bleaching; increased after pH cycle but not significantly
outcomes.clinical_significance: Bleaching reduces enamel microhardness; remineralization may partially recover it

outcomes.result: Decreased after discoloration (P>0.05); increased significantly after pH cycle with significant differences between 4th measurement and other measurements (P<0.05)
outcomes.clinical_significance: pH cycling with remineralizing toothpaste alters surface roughness of bleached enamel

notes: The abstract contains a likely typographical error: Group 1 bleaching agent concentration is not specified (text reads 'Group 1: Group 2: Samples treated with Beka HP 30%'). It is inferred that Group 1 used a lower concentration (possibly 16% HP) based on the study design mentioning 'two different concentrations of hydrogen peroxide'. Only 30% HP (Beka HP) is explicitly named.
source_citation: Kopuz, D., Erçin, Ö., Tağtekin, D., & Yanikoğlu, F. (2025). Effect of remineralization product on the microhardness and surface roughness of enamel after bleaching agents. American Journal of Dentistry. PubMed ID: 40000001.
adverse_events: Reduction in enamel microhardness following hydrogen peroxide bleaching
Alteration of enamel surface roughness following bleaching

drugs_mentioned.name: hydrogen peroxide
drugs_mentioned.dental_relevance: Used as a bleaching agent at two concentrations; causes reduction in enamel microhardness and surface changes

drugs_mentioned.name: sodium fluoride
drugs_mentioned.dental_relevance: Present in toothpaste used during pH cycling after bleaching; may have a positive effect on enamel microhardness recovery

perioperative_consideration: Application of sodium fluoride-containing remineralizing toothpaste after bleaching may help partially recover enamel microhardness; clinicians should consider recommending remineralizing agents post-bleaching.
uncertainties: Group 1 bleaching agent concentration is not specified in the abstract due to apparent typographical error; only 30% HP (Group 2, Beka HP) is explicitly named.
Full text not available; exact protocol for pH cycling and remineralization product details are not fully described.
Statistical reporting appears inconsistent in abstract (P<0.05 used to indicate both significance and non-significance); interpretation based on context.
No conflict of interest or funding information available in the provided data.
